title: Birdwatching Mount Mulanje: Routes, Species & Seasonal Guide
meta_title: Birdwatching Mount Mulanje: Routes, Species & Seasonal Guide
meta_description: Plan your birdwatching Mount Mulanje trip — 180+ species, best trails, seasons, and where to stay. Spot the Thyolo Alethe and rare endemics.
author: The Thyolo House
excerpt: Mount Mulanje is one of southern Africa's most rewarding birding destinations, with over 300 species recorded across Afro-montane forests, plateau grasslands, and river gorges. This guide covers the key species, best routes, seasonal timing, and where to stay — including the lowland forests of Thyolo just down the road.
content:
Most birders heading to Malawi make straight for the lakeshore, where fish eagles call from every bay and bee-eaters flash through the riparian scrub. They are not wrong — Lake Malawi is magnificent. But the country's most concentrated pocket of rare and endemic forest birds sits further south, on and around a granite massif that rises more than two thousand metres above the tea estates of the Shire Highlands. Birdwatching Mount Mulanje puts you in reach of species you simply cannot find anywhere else on the continent — the critically rare Thyolo Alethe, the luminous Yellow-throated Apalis, forest raptors that hunt through cathedral-like canopies — all within a landscape that was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in July 2025.

With approximately 300 species recorded within the Mount Mulanje Forest Reserve (an officially designated Important Bird Area), this single massif rivals birding sites ten times its fame. The difference is that you will often have the trails to yourself. This guide covers the birds, the routes, the seasons, and the practical details you need to plan a birding trip to Mulanje — plus the lowland forests of Thyolo, just twenty minutes from The Thyolo House, that many birders overlook entirely.

Why Mount Mulanje Is Southern Africa's Hidden Birding Hotspot

Mount Mulanje is an inselberg — an isolated massif that rises abruptly from the surrounding plains to a peak altitude of 3,002 metres at Sapitwa. That isolation is the key to its birdlife. Over millennia, the mountain's Afro-montane forests became refugia for species that retreated upward as the lowland climate changed, creating pockets of endemism found nowhere else in southern Africa.

The numbers alone are striking. Over 300 bird species have been recorded on and around the mountain, a figure that includes a significant number of Afro-montane specialists — birds confined to the shrinking patches of evergreen forest that cling to the massif between roughly 900 and 2,000 metres. BirdLife International has designated the entire Mulanje Mountain Forest Reserve as an Important Bird Area, recognising its global significance for threatened and range-restricted species.

Compare this to better-known birding destinations in the region. The Nyika Plateau in northern Malawi is excellent for grassland raptors and has its own IBA status, but it lacks Mulanje's dense montane forest — and the forest endemics that come with it. South Africa's Kruger or Hwange in Zimbabwe offer far larger species lists overall, but almost nothing in the Afro-montane endemic category. For forest birding in southern Africa, Mulanje stands in a class of its own, comparable in significance to the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania but far less visited.

The Birds You Came For

Every birder arriving at Mulanje has a target list. Here are the species that justify the journey — and that make birdwatching Mount Mulanje a genuinely world-class experience.

Thyolo Alethe (Alethe choloensis) — The #1 Target

This is the bird that puts Mulanje on the global birding map. The Thyolo Alethe is classified as Endangered by the IUCN, with a total population estimated at fewer than 2,500 individuals. It exists only on a handful of isolated montane forest patches in southeastern Malawi and adjacent northeastern Mozambique, between 950 and 1,900 metres elevation. A small, secretive bird — just 17 to 20 centimetres — it is cinnamon-brown above with a distinctive white throat and grey breast. It feeds on the forest floor, flicking through leaf litter for insects, and its quiet habits make it a genuine challenge to spot.

The good news for birders is that the Thyolo Alethe moves to lower elevations between March and September, when it can be found near the Ruo River at more accessible altitudes. During the breeding season (October–February), it retreats deeper into the mid-altitude forest. Either way, patience and a good guide are essential. Listen for its soft, repetitive call — a series of clear whistled notes, often given from dense undergrowth.

Yellow-throated Apalis

A jewel of the montane canopy — vibrant lime-green upperparts, a bright yellow throat, and the kind of restless energy that makes warblers both delightful and maddening to watch through binoculars. This apalis is confined to montane forests in Malawi and a narrow band of adjacent Mozambique, and Mulanje is one of the most reliable places to encounter it. Look for mixed-species flocks working through the mid-canopy in the Ruo Gorge and Lichenya area.

White-winged Apalis (Apalis chariessa macphersoni)

Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN, this distinctive apalis is found in the Mulanje and Shire Highlands forests. It tends to favour forest edges and gaps rather than the deep interior, so it can often be picked up along trails and at the transition zones between forest and tea plantation.

Spotted Ground Thrush (belcheri subspecies)

Once found more widely on Mulanje, this Endangered subspecies is now probably restricted to Chisongeli forest on the mountain's southern flanks. It appears to have gone locally extinct in the Ruo Gorge, where it was formerly regular. Finding it requires a dedicated visit to Chisongeli with a knowledgeable local guide — and a good measure of luck.

Other Forest Specialists

Beyond the headline species, the Mulanje forests support a rich supporting cast:

  • Green-headed Oriole — a stunning montane forest species, emerald and gold, often heard before seen
  • Red-faced Crimsonwing — skulks in dense undergrowth near streams; patience required
  • Malawi Batis (Cape Batis) — common in the forest mid-storey, part of the ubiquitous mixed flocks
  • Stripe-cheeked Greenbul — a canopy species, more often heard than seen
  • Silvery-cheeked Hornbill — enormous and unmistakable at forest edges, with a loud, braying call
  • Bronze-naped Pigeon — a shy montane forest pigeon, best spotted at dawn perched high in the canopy
  • Green Twinspot — a tiny, exquisite finch of the forest floor, jewelled with white spots on dark green
  • Mountain Wagtail — found along every mountain stream, bobbing on boulders in the current

Raptors of the Plateau

Once you climb above the treeline onto Mulanje's plateau grasslands, the birding shifts entirely. The open ground and dramatic cliff faces attract an impressive raptor community: Black Eagle, Crowned Eagle (hunting in the forest below but often seen soaring above), Peregrine Falcon, Lanner Falcon, African Marsh Harrier, Black-breasted Snake Eagle, and — on rare occasions — Martial Eagle. The Chambe Basin, with its towering rock faces, is particularly productive for raptors.

Best Birdwatching Routes on Mount Mulanje

Mulanje's size — the massif covers roughly 650 square kilometres — means you need to choose your routes strategically. Different habitats produce different species, and no single trail gives you everything. Here are the four key birding routes, each targeting a different niche.

Ruo Gorge — The Forest Cathedral

The Ruo Gorge, on the mountain's eastern side, is the single most productive birding site on Mulanje. A deep, humid gorge cut by the Ruo River, it supports some of the densest and least-disturbed Afro-montane forest on the massif. This is prime habitat for the Thyolo Alethe (especially March–September when they descend to the gorge floor), Yellow-throated Apalis, Green-headed Oriole, and Malawi Batis. The canopy is high and continuous, so a neck-saving pair of binoculars with good close-focus is essential.

Trail details: The Ruo Gorge trail starts from the Ruo Forest Station and follows the river upstream. The path is moderately difficult — expect uneven terrain, some scrambling over rocks, and river crossings that may be tricky in the wet season. Allow a full day for serious birding here. An early start (before 6:00 AM) is essential to catch the dawn chorus.

Likabula Forest — Thyolo Alethe Territory

The Likabula area on the mountain's western side is the most accessible approach to Mulanje, and the forest here is another reliable site for the Thyolo Alethe. The trail to Likabula Falls passes through a mix of plantation forest and natural woodland before reaching patches of genuine Afro-montane forest. It is less dramatic than the Ruo Gorge but easier to navigate, making it a good choice for birders who want forest species without the more demanding terrain.

Trail details: Moderate difficulty, 3–5 hours for a birding circuit. The lower sections are well-maintained. The MMCT (Mulanje Mountain Conservation Trust) forestry office is located here and can help arrange guides. Permit fees apply — currently around MWK 5,000 (approximately USD $3) for foreign visitors.

Lichenya Plateau — Montane Grassland Specialists

The Lichenya Plateau sits at roughly 2,000 metres on the mountain's southeastern side and offers a completely different birding experience. Up here, the forest gives way to rolling Afro-montane grassland studded with proteas and heather. The bird community shifts accordingly — cisticolas, pipits, stonechats, and sunbirds replace the forest skulkers, while raptors patrol the cliff edges. This is also where the critically endangered Mulanje cedar (Widdringtonia whytei) survives in scattered stands, adding botanical interest to the birding.

Trail details: Reaching Lichenya requires a steep 4–5 hour ascent from Likhubula or an approach via the Fort Lister Gap. Most birders combine it with an overnight stay at the Lichenya Hut, which allows for dawn birding on the plateau before descending. The hut is basic but functional — bring a sleeping bag and food.

Chambe Basin — Raptors and Rock-dwellers

The Chambe Basin, beneath the massive Chambe Peak face on the western side of the massif, is the place for raptors. Black Eagles nest on the cliffs, Peregrine Falcons hunt from the rock faces, and Crowned Eagles can sometimes be seen riding the thermals that build against the wall. The basin also supports some forest patches at its lower edges where forest species overlap with the open-ground birds. It is a physically demanding approach — allow a full day to reach the basin and return — but rewarding for raptor enthusiasts.

Trail details: The Chambe Plateau path is the steepest common route on Mulanje. Start from Likhubula and allow 5–6 hours for the ascent. Not suitable for casual walkers — this is a proper mountain hike with significant altitude gain.

When to Go — Peak Season, Weather & Timing

Timing matters enormously for birdwatching Mount Mulanje. The mountain creates its own microclimate, and the birding experience changes dramatically between seasons.

Dry Season (May–October)

This is the most popular window for visitors, and for good reason. Trails are drier and safer, the skies are generally clear in the morning (cloud builds in the afternoon), and the reduced foliage makes birds easier to spot. Temperatures on the plateau drop to near freezing at night, so warm layers are essential for overnight trips.

For birders specifically, the dry season has a crucial advantage: the Thyolo Alethe descends to lower elevations near the Ruo River from March through September, making it more accessible. Mixed-species flocks also tend to be more cohesive and predictable in the cooler months, which makes forest birding more efficient — find one flock and you may pick up ten species in twenty minutes.

Breeding Season (November–April)

The rains transform Mulanje. The forest turns impossibly green, streams become torrents, and the birds breed. Males sing more vigorously and conspicuously, making identification easier — you will hear species in the breeding season that are virtually silent the rest of the year. Migrant species from further north also arrive, swelling the species list.

The trade-off is practical: trails are muddy and sometimes dangerous, river crossings can become impassable, and afternoon downpours are virtually guaranteed. Leeches are more common in the wet forest. The Thyolo Alethe retreats to higher elevations, making it harder to reach. If you can tolerate the conditions, November and December — early in the rains, when breeding activity peaks but before the heaviest rainfall — offer an excellent compromise.

Daily Timing

The dawn chorus on Mulanje starts early — between 5:00 and 5:30 AM depending on the season. This first hour of light is by far the most productive birding window, especially in the forest where activity drops off sharply by mid-morning. Plan to be on the trail before dawn. A second, smaller peak of activity occurs in the late afternoon from around 4:00 PM until dusk. The middle of the day is generally quiet in the forest, though raptors on the plateau are most visible during the heat when thermals are strongest.

Birding the Thyolo Forest Reserve

Here is the secret that experienced Malawi birders know: some of the best forest birding in the country is not on Mulanje itself but in the lowland forest patches scattered across the Thyolo district, just twenty minutes from The Thyolo House. These fragments of indigenous forest — nestled among the tea estates at around 1,000 metres elevation — harbour their own populations of montane forest species, including the Thyolo Alethe (the bird is, after all, named for this district).

The Thyolo Forest Reserve and smaller patches on private estates like the Conforzi property offer forest birding at a fraction of the effort required on Mulanje. There is no steep ascent, no multi-day commitment, and no need for mountain gear. You walk from your room at The Thyolo House, cross through the tea plantation, and enter the forest. Green-headed Orioles call from the canopy. Stripe-cheeked Greenbuls work through the mid-storey. If you are very fortunate and very patient, you may pick up the Alethe in the undergrowth.

For birders planning a Mulanje trip, the strategic value is clear: spend a day or two birding the Thyolo forests at a comfortable altitude, ticking off forest species in gentler conditions, then head to Mulanje for the mountain-specific targets. Many of the Afro-montane species overlap between the two sites, so you arrive at Mulanje with a head start on your list and can focus your mountain time on the species that genuinely require altitude.

The estate gardens themselves are productive too. Sunbirds work the bougainvillea and aloes, weavers build in the palms, and the open lawn areas attract wagtails and shrikes. It is pleasant, low-effort birding over breakfast — the kind of morning that reminds you why you took up the hobby in the first place.

Where to Stay for Birdwatching Mount Mulanje

Your choice of accommodation shapes the entire trip. Serious birders will want a combination of mountain huts for overnight access to high-altitude species and a comfortable lowland base for recovery days and Thyolo forest birding.

Mountain Huts

The MMCT maintains a network of huts across the Mulanje plateau, including Lichenya Hut, Chambe Hut, and several others. These are basic shelters — expect wooden bunks, no electricity, and no running water. You bring your own sleeping bag, food, and cooking gear. What they offer is priceless access: you sleep on the mountain and bird at dawn without the three-hour approach walk. Book through the MMCT forestry office at Likhubula. Hut fees are modest — around MWK 5,000–10,000 per person per night.

Likhubula Forestry Area

The MMCT has basic accommodation near the Likhubula Forest Station at the base of the mountain. This is the most practical base for accessing the Likabula and Chambe routes. Facilities are simple but adequate, and the birding starts from the doorstep — the surrounding forest and plantation edges are productive in their own right.

The Thyolo House — Your Lowland Base

For comfort, food, and lowland forest birding, The Thyolo House is the natural choice. Sitting on the historic Conforzi Tea Estate in Thyolo — roughly 40 minutes' drive from the Likhubula trailhead — it offers five boutique rooms, an Italian-fusion restaurant using estate-grown ingredients, a swimming pool, and direct access to both tea plantation walks and indigenous forest trails.

The practical logic works well: base yourself at The Thyolo House for the first and last nights of your trip, using the comfortable rooms and excellent food to bookend the rougher mountain days. Spend your Thyolo days birding the estate forest and the Thyolo Forest Reserve. Then head to Mulanje for one or two nights in the mountain huts, returning to the estate for a hot shower, a proper meal, and a cold drink by the pool. Several visiting birders have told us this combination — lowland forest comfort plus mountain hut immersion — is the ideal format for a Mulanje birding trip.

The Thyolo House is also a sensible base for day trips to Mount Mulanje. If you prefer not to sleep on the mountain, you can drive to Likhubula early in the morning, bird the lower forest trails for a full day, and return to the estate for dinner. You will miss the plateau species, but the forest birding at Likabula and along the Ruo approach is excellent even without an overnight stay.

Practical Tips for Birding Mount Mulanje

Hire a Bird Guide

This is not optional — it is essential. Local guides know where the key species are, which trails are safe, and how to navigate the mountain's notoriously fast-changing weather. For the Thyolo Alethe specifically, a guide who knows the current territories can save you days of fruitless searching. The MMCT forestry office at Likhubula can arrange mountain guides (expect to pay around MWK 10,000–20,000 per day depending on the route). For specialist birding guides who know the target species and their calls, ask at the forestry office or contact the Malawi Birding Society for recommendations. Some guides based in Blantyre and Zomba lead dedicated Mulanje birding trips.

Field Guides and Checklists

The essential field guide is Newman's Birds of Southern Africa or Sasol Birds of Southern Africa — both cover Malawi's species comprehensively. For a Mulanje-specific checklist, Avibase maintains a complete species list for the Mulanje Mountain Forest Reserve (available free online at avibase.bsc-eoc.org). Download or print this before you go and use it to plan your target species by habitat and elevation.

Permits and Fees

All visitors to Mount Mulanje Forest Reserve need a permit from the MMCT, obtainable at the Likhubula forestry office. Foreign visitor entry fees are approximately MWK 5,000 (around USD $3). Guide fees are additional. Mountain hut fees range from MWK 5,000–10,000 per person per night. Carry cash — card payment is not available at the forestry office.

What to Pack

  • Binoculars: 8×42 or 10×42 with good close-focus for forest birding. Waterproof is strongly advisable.
  • Spotting scope: Useful on the plateau for raptors but cumbersome in the forest — leave it at base unless you are specifically targeting grassland or cliff species.
  • Camera: A long lens (300mm+) is needed for bird photography in the forest canopy. Conditions are often low-light, so a fast lens or high-ISO capability helps.
  • Clothing: Muted greens and browns. Avoid white or bright colours in the forest. Layers are critical — it can be warm at the base and near freezing on the plateau overnight.
  • Rain gear: A packable waterproof jacket is non-negotiable, even in the dry season. The mountain generates its own cloud and mist (the famous chiperone).
  • Footwear: Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support and good grip. Trails are rocky, uneven, and frequently muddy.
  • Headlamp: For pre-dawn starts and hut nights.
  • Notebook and pen: For field notes — phone batteries die; notebooks do not.

Combining Birding with Other Activities

A birding trip to Mulanje does not have to be single-minded. The mountain and surrounding area offer plenty for non-birding travel companions or for your own rest days. Tea estate tours in Thyolo are a gentle half-day activity that pairs well with an afternoon of garden birding. The Thyolo House offers art workshops with owner Flavia Conforzi, a pool for lazy afternoons, and some of the best food in southern Malawi. Mount Mulanje itself is a world-class hiking destination — many birders combine species-hunting with the challenge of summiting Sapitwa Peak or traversing the plateau.

The UNESCO World Heritage inscription in July 2025 has raised the mountain's international profile, and the MMCT is working to improve trail infrastructure and visitor facilities. Now is an excellent time to visit — the birding is as good as it has ever been, and the mountain is gaining the recognition (and conservation funding) it deserves, while remaining blissfully uncrowded.

Ready to plan your birdwatching Mount Mulanje trip? We are happy to help with logistics, transfers from Blantyre, and advice on combining mountain birding with time on the estate. Message us on WhatsApp or email thethyolohouse@gmail.com to start planning.
